serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Rogan Josh is approximately 250g, comprising braised mutton pieces in a rich spiced gravy, usually served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Rogan Josh — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Rogan Josh (record G00010384). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this canonical source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Spiced Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Rogan Josh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry entries in the Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 7.44g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly from tomatoes, onions, and spices; the high protein (14.05g) and fat (11.58g) content further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 250g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg per 100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 1168mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2000mg daily DASH limit in a single dish.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150g, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles, papadums, or salted chutneys to keep cumulative daily sodium within DASH diet thresholds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44g/100g) with moderate sugar (4.69g/100g); high protein (14.05g/100g) from mutton slows gastric emptying and moderates post-prandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Rogan Josh is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200g) without high-glycaemic accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the estimated GI of 35 and GL of approximately 4–7 per serving support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.2g/100g from mutton and cooking oil, cholesterol 85mg/100g, and high sodium 467mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk, particularly with regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Rogan Josh to occasional consumption (once weekly or less), choose lean mutton cuts, request reduced oil in preparation, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which directly amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; saturated fat (~4.2g/100g) and trace trans fat (0.1g/100g) add to pro-inflammatory burden; tomatoes as a nightshade ingredient may trigger symptom flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Rogan Josh due to its high arachidonic acid content from red meat and the presence of nightshade ingredients (tomatoes); substituting with omega-3-rich protein sources is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Rogan Josh is a classic slow-braised red meat curry originating from the Kashmir region of India, widely consumed across South Asia and popular in Singaporean Indian restaurants. The dish is prepared by marinating bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in yogurt and whole spices, then slow-cooking them in a rich, deeply coloured gravy made from Kashmiri red chillies, tomatoes, onions, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves. The result is tender, fall-off-the-bone mutton in a thick, intensely spiced, mildly tangy sauce with a characteristic deep red-orange colour derived from Kashmiri chilli rather than artificial colouring. The flavour profile is complex — warm, earthy, mildly spicy, and slightly sour from yogurt and tomato — with a rich, unctuous mouthfeel from the rendered mutton fat and cooking oil. Per 100g, the dish provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat (approximately 4.2g saturated), 7.44g carbohydrate, 4.69g sugar, 0.3g dietary fibre, and 467mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is low-GI (estimated GI 35) and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions, but its high sodium content (467mg/100g) makes it a concern for hypertension and DASH diet adherence. The saturated fat and cholesterol (85mg/100g) warrant moderation for cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore contexts and gluten-free. It is commonly served with basmati rice, naan, or roti as part of a lunch or dinner meal.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 4.2g/100g based on USDA SR Legacy data for cooked mutton curry as a cross-reference; trans fat 0.1g/100g is a trace estimate consistent with ruminant animal fat. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes. All other fields are grounded in approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mutton Rogan Josh as a prepared dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared dish is not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient panel beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the primary source for this dish as of April 2026. saturated_fat_g (4.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked mutton data as the HPB source does not report fatty acid fractions; trans_fat_g (0.1g/100g) is a trace estimate consistent with ruminant animal fat composition. Cultural classification corrected from 'Middle Eastern' (input) to 'South Asian / Indian' — Rogan Josh is a Kashmiri dish of Persian-Mughal origin, not Middle Eastern. GI estimated at 35 (low) using analogous meat curry entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Rogan Josh was found in the database as of April 2026.
